validation.comparison_result: 2 rows have floating-point precision errors in sum_disc_price calculation (0.01 difference). This is likely due to floating-point accumulation order in parallel aggregation.

Q1.notes: Performance is excellent (89.4 ms vs DuckDB's 86.4 ms best time), but validation fails due to minor floating-point precision errors in sum_disc_price. The scan_filter phase dominates at 60.5% of total time.

Q1.bottleneck: The scan_filter phase takes 54.1 ms out of 89.4 ms total. While this is already very fast (comparable to DuckDB), there's still room for optimization through SIMD vectorization and zone map pruning. The data is sorted by l_shipdate and zone maps exist but are not being used.
Q1.root_cause: Current implementation uses thread parallelism effectively (64 cores) with morsel-driven execution, but lacks SIMD vectorization for the date comparison (shipdate <= cutoff) which processes 59.9M rows. Additionally, the zone map index on l_shipdate is not being utilized to skip blocks where max(shipdate) <= cutoff_date. The floating-point error is caused by non-deterministic accumulation order in parallel threads.

analysis.overall: Q1 achieves excellent performance at 89.4 ms (within 3.5% of DuckDB's best time and 168x faster than PostgreSQL), but fails validation due to floating-point precision errors. The scan_filter operation dominates at 60.5% of execution time. Two main optimization opportunities: (1) Fix floating-point accumulation for correctness, (2) Add SIMD vectorization and zone map pruning to reduce scan_filter time from 54.1 ms to potentially 30-40 ms.

critical_fixes.issue: Floating-point precision error in sum_disc_price calculation
critical_fixes.fix: Use Kahan summation algorithm for compensated floating-point accumulation
critical_fixes.description: The current parallel aggregation accumulates floating-point values in non-deterministic order across threads, leading to precision errors. Implement Kahan (compensated) summation in the AggregateResult struct for sum_disc_price and sum_charge calculations. This maintains an error compensation term alongside each sum to preserve precision. For each thread-local accumulation: temp = value - compensation; new_sum = sum + temp; compensation = (new_sum - sum) - temp. This ensures deterministic, high-precision results regardless of accumulation order.

performance_optimizations.description: The storage design includes a zone map index on l_shipdate (sorted column with 100K block size), but the current code doesn't use it. Load the shipdate_zonemap.bin file and skip entire 100K-row blocks where max_shipdate > cutoff_date (10472). Given the cutoff is 1998-09-03 and data spans 1992-2018 to 1998-12-30, roughly 2% of blocks can be skipped (those with min > cutoff). Implementation: (1) mmap lineitem/l_shipdate_zonemap.bin containing ZoneMapEntry structs (24 bytes each), (2) In the parallel scan loop, check zone maps before processing each 100K block, (3) Skip blocks where zone.min_val > cutoff_date. Since data is sorted by shipdate, most qualifying rows are in early blocks, allowing late blocks to be skipped entirely. Expected reduction: 1-2% of I/O, saving 1-2 ms from scan_filter time.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduce scan_filter from 54.1 ms to ~52-53 ms (2-4% improvement, ~1-2 ms savings). Zone maps provide block-level pruning with minimal overhead.

performance_optimizations.description: The inner scan loop performs 59.9M date comparisons (shipdate[i] <= cutoff_date) sequentially. Use AVX2 SIMD to compare 8 int32 date values simultaneously. Implementation: (1) Include immintrin.h for AVX2 intrinsics, (2) In the morsel processing loop, process shipdate in chunks of 8 using _mm256_loadu_si256 to load 8 dates, (3) Use _mm256_set1_epi32(cutoff_date) for broadcast threshold, (4) Compare with _mm256_cmpgt_epi32 to get mask, (5) Use _mm256_movemask_ps to extract bitmask of qualifying rows, (6) Process qualifying rows for aggregation. This reduces comparison overhead by 8x. With 64 cores and AVX2 support (confirmed in workload_analysis), expect 15-25% speedup on scan_filter phase. Handle tail elements (row_count % 8) with scalar code.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduce scan_filter from 54.1 ms to ~42-46 ms (15-22% improvement, ~8-12 ms savings). SIMD provides near-linear speedup for predicate evaluation.

performance_optimizations.description: The aggregation loop performs 59.1M floating-point arithmetic operations (disc_price = price * (1 - disc), charge = disc_price * (1 + tax), plus 7 accumulations per row). Vectorize these with AVX2 double-precision operations (_mm256d for 4 doubles at once). Implementation: (1) After filtering with SIMD date mask, process qualifying rows in batches of 4, (2) Use _mm256_loadu_pd to load quantity/price/discount/tax arrays, (3) Compute disc_price and charge with vectorized FMA operations (_mm256_fmadd_pd, _mm256_fmsub_pd), (4) Accumulate into SIMD register accumulators, (5) Horizontally reduce accumulators at end of morsel. This reduces arithmetic overhead and improves instruction-level parallelism. Expected 10-15% speedup on the arithmetic-heavy aggregation path within scan_filter.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduce scan_filter from 54.1 ms to ~48-51 ms (6-11% improvement, ~3-6 ms savings when combined with other optimizations). Note: Must preserve Kahan summation for correctness.

performance_optimizations.description: The current code uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL for mmap hints, but doesn't explicitly prefetch data for each morsel. With 64-thread parallelism, memory access patterns become less sequential and more random across threads. Add explicit prefetching: (1) In each thread's morsel loop, use __builtin_prefetch or _mm_prefetch to prefetch the next morsel's data while processing the current one, (2) Prefetch all 7 column arrays (shipdate, returnflag, linestatus, quantity, extendedprice, discount, tax) with temporal locality hints (PREFETCH_T0 for L1 cache), (3) Prefetch 2-3 cache lines ahead (128-192 bytes) to hide memory latency. On HDD storage with large dataset, this helps keep the CPU fed with data. Expected 5-10% improvement in memory-bound scenarios.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduce scan_filter from 54.1 ms to ~51-52 ms (2-5% improvement, ~2-3 ms savings). Prefetching helps hide memory latency on HDD-backed storage.

performance_optimizations.description: The current filter implementation uses a branching if statement (if (shipdate[i] <= cutoff_date)) which may suffer from branch misprediction since 98.5% of rows match (very high selectivity). However, for such high selectivity, branches typically predict well. Consider branch-free filtering only if combined with SIMD (already in priority 2). Alternative: Use arithmetic-based selection vector generation where match_mask = (shipdate[i] <= cutoff_date) produces 0/1, then materialize qualifying indices. This is more beneficial for medium selectivity (20-80%) predicates. For Q1 with 98.5% selectivity, the branch predictor should handle this well, so this is lower priority.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Minimal impact for Q1 (high selectivity predicate). Estimated 0-2% improvement (~0-1 ms). More useful for queries with lower selectivity filters.

performance_optimizations.technique: Thread Count Tuning and Morsel Size Optimization
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation uses std::thread::hardware_concurrency() (64 cores) with 100K morsel size. However, with 59.9M rows and 64 threads, each thread processes ~10 morsels (937K rows). The morsel size of 100K may not be optimal for the L3 cache (44 MB total, ~700 KB per core). Experiment with: (1) Morsel size: Try 50K-200K rows to find sweet spot between cache utilization and load balancing, (2) Thread count: Try 48-56 threads instead of 64 to reduce scheduling overhead and leave cores for OS/background tasks. With 7 columns × 8 bytes/column × 100K rows = 5.6 MB per morsel (exceeds per-core L3), consider smaller morsels (50K = 2.8 MB fits better). Profile with different configurations to find optimal balance.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduce scan_filter from 54.1 ms to ~52-54 ms (0-4% improvement, ~0-2 ms savings). Tuning is workload-dependent and requires experimentation.

summary: Q1 iteration 0 achieves excellent performance (89.4 ms, within 3.5% of DuckDB's 86.4 ms best time) but fails validation due to floating-point precision errors. Critical fix required: Implement Kahan summation for compensated accumulation. Top performance optimization: Add SIMD vectorized date filtering (priority 2) which can reduce scan_filter time by 15-22% (~8-12 ms). Zone map block skipping (priority 1) provides additional 2-4% gains with minimal risk. Combined optimizations could bring total time to ~75-80 ms range, matching or exceeding DuckDB's performance while maintaining correctness.
